a00be301-0e39-42d5-b4ec-c1f0f2877821.name: Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act (FD&C Act)
a00be301-0e39-42d5-b4ec-c1f0f2877821.citation: Pub. L. 75-717, 52 Stat. 1040; codified at 21 U.S.C. § 301 et seq.
a00be301-0e39-42d5-b4ec-c1f0f2877821.description:
The FD&C Act is the foundational federal statute for FDA's authority over food (as well as drugs, medical devices, and cosmetics). It prohibits introducing "adulterated" or "misbranded" food into interstate commerce, and those two terms — adulteration and misbranding — are the legal hooks nearly every downstream FDA food rule hangs on. Adulteration covers contamination, unsafe substances, and unsanitary production conditions; misbranding covers false or misleading labeling and failure to declare required information.

Virtually every food manufacturer, importer, and distributor operating in the US is subject to it, whether or not they ever interact with a more specific rule. It has been amended repeatedly since 1938 rather than replaced, and FSMA itself operates by amending this Act rather than standing alone.

Because it predates modern preventive-controls thinking, the Act's original text says relatively little about HACCP-style systems or supply-chain verification — those obligations were layered on by FSMA and by earlier HACCP rules for meat, poultry, seafood, and juice, each also codified as amendments or companion regulations under this same statutory umbrella.

2f72c0de-4634-4798-9aca-4d01ec2c0084.name: Food Safety Modernization Act (FSMA)
2f72c0de-4634-4798-9aca-4d01ec2c0084.description:
FSMA is the most significant overhaul of US food safety law since 1938, shifting food safety from a reactive, respond-to-outbreaks model to a preventive one: facilities are required to identify hazards in advance and put controls in place to prevent them, rather than FDA responding only after contaminated product has already reached the public.

FSMA itself is an amendment to the FD&C Act, not a freestanding code — it directed FDA to write seven major implementing rules covering preventive controls for human food (PCHF), preventive controls for animal food (PCAF), produce safety, foreign supplier verification (FSVP), sanitary transportation, intentional adulteration, and food traceability (FSMA 204). Each of those is its own regulation with its own compliance dates and applicability rules; FSMA is the umbrella statute that created the requirement for them to exist.

Who is covered depends entirely on which implementing rule applies — a domestic finished-goods manufacturer, a produce farm, and an importer of food ingredients are all "under FSMA" but each answers to a different rule with different obligations.

4db45158-1d5c-4b87-86ad-c022454a603e.name: Preventive Controls for Human Food (PCHF)
4db45158-1d5c-4b87-86ad-c022454a603e.description:
PCHF is the rule most food manufacturers actually live under day to day. It requires a facility to write and maintain a Food Safety Plan, and it names who is allowed to write one: a Preventive Controls Qualified Individual (PCQI), a role defined by training or equivalent job experience rather than a license, most commonly satisfied by completing the FDA-recognized FSPCA standardized curriculum.

The plan itself has six required components (see Fields below), and the rule also folds in updated Current Good Manufacturing Practices (CGMPs) covering personnel hygiene, plant and grounds sanitation, and equipment design — the baseline conditions a preventive-controls system is built on top of.

Reanalysis is required at least every 3 years or whenever a change could introduce a new hazard (21 CFR 117.170). Very small businesses (under a revenue threshold), farms, and retail/restaurant operations are exempt or fall under modified requirements instead.

fields.name: Hazard analysis
fields.description: Written identification of biological, chemical (including radiological), and physical hazards that could affect the food, for each step in the process.

fields.name: Preventive controls
fields.description: The specific controls applied to significant hazards — process controls (e.g. a CCP-style cook step), sanitation controls, allergen controls, and supply-chain controls.

fields.name: Monitoring
fields.description: Procedures that confirm preventive controls are consistently performed as designed.

fields.name: Corrective actions
fields.description: Documented steps taken when a preventive control isn't properly implemented, including evaluating affected product and correcting the cause.

fields.name: Verification
fields.description: Activities beyond monitoring that confirm preventive controls are actually working (e.g. calibration checks, environmental monitoring, record review).

fields.name: Recall plan
fields.description: Written procedures for the facility to act on if a hazard requiring control is identified — how a recall would actually be executed.

2daacfdd-b270-4df6-ad79-bd7a0af491f6.name: Preventive Controls for Animal Food (PCAF)
2daacfdd-b270-4df6-ad79-bd7a0af491f6.description:
Part 507 is the animal-food counterpart to Preventive Controls for Human Food (PCHF): it applies the same risk-based framework to facilities that manufacture, process, pack, or hold food for animals, from pet food and treats to livestock and poultry feed. Covered facilities must conduct a hazard analysis and, where a hazard requires one, implement preventive controls with associated monitoring, verification, and corrective-action procedures.

The rule also carries Current Good Manufacturing Practices (CGMPs) tailored to animal food production, adapted from the human-food CGMPs but reflecting that finished product is feed rather than food for people. Facilities must document their food safety plan in writing and keep it on-site, subject to FDA inspection.

Small and very small businesses get qualified exemptions or modified requirements similar to those under PCHF, and there is a separate supply-chain program requirement when a hazard is controlled by a supplier rather than the receiving facility itself.

bbacc25c-33bb-407e-bb70-c6e7a67f8784.name: Produce Safety Rule (PSR)
bbacc25c-33bb-407e-bb70-c6e7a67f8784.description:
The Produce Safety Rule sets federal, science-based minimum standards for the safe growing, harvesting, packing, and holding of fruits and vegetables that are typically eaten raw, applying at the farm level rather than to processing facilities. It covers agricultural water quality, use of biological soil amendments of animal origin, worker health and hygiene training, animal intrusion into growing areas, and sanitary design of equipment and buildings.

The rule does not apply to produce destined for commercial processing (e.g., canning) that includes an adequate reduction of microorganisms, and farms below certain size thresholds qualify for exemptions or modified requirements.

FDA enforcement is primarily through on-farm inspections, often delegated to state departments of agriculture under cooperative agreements, rather than the desk audits typical of facility-based rules. Because this rule governs the growing/harvesting end of the supply chain, it sits upstream of the receiving-through-shipping scope this dataset otherwise focuses on, but it shapes what documentation a receiving facility may require from produce suppliers.

ed70a1f5-bbb6-4ae3-96e3-0bea4d58dc9a.name: Foreign Supplier Verification Program (FSVP)
ed70a1f5-bbb6-4ae3-96e3-0bea4d58dc9a.description:
FSVP shifts the compliance burden onto the US importer of record rather than the foreign supplier FDA can't directly inspect. Before an importer can bring in a food, they must determine what hazards require a control at the supplier, verify the supplier is actually controlling them, and keep records showing that verification happened. Verification can mean an audit, sampling/testing, or review of the supplier's food safety records — the appropriate method depends on the hazard and the supplier's track record.

This work must be performed or overseen by a qualified individual under 21 CFR 1.503, qualified by training or job experience, distinct from but often the same person as a facility's PCQI. FSVP applies per-importer, per-food — a company importing from multiple foreign suppliers needs a hazard analysis and verification plan for each.

41dc8af7-974a-4eaf-b4de-eacb5109dfb7.name: Sanitary Transportation Rule
41dc8af7-974a-4eaf-b4de-eacb5109dfb7.description:
This rule addresses a gap that HACCP-style plant controls don't cover: contamination or temperature abuse in the vehicle between facilities. It puts specific, different obligations on each party in the transport chain — a shipper must specify in writing (21 CFR 1.908) whether the carrier or shipper is responsible for pre-cooling the vehicle and maintaining temperature in transit; a carrier must keep vehicles and transport equipment in sanitary condition and follow the temperature controls the shipper specifies; a receiver must check for signs of temperature abuse or contamination on arrival.

It applies to shelf-stable, frozen, and refrigerated food alike, though the practical burden — reefer unit monitoring, pre-cool verification, in-transit temperature logging — falls heaviest on temperature-sensitive product. Rail carriers, shippers by rail, and certain small businesses have modified or exempt status.

3eecd978-8139-4ab1-948f-828c00c79f63.name: Intentional Adulteration Rule (Food Defense)
3eecd978-8139-4ab1-948f-828c00c79f63.description:
The Intentional Adulteration rule is the FSMA rule aimed not at accidental contamination but at deliberate, large-scale attacks on the food supply — economically motivated tampering or terrorism, not a sick worker or a dirty conveyor. It applies to large domestic and foreign food facilities and requires each to develop and maintain a written Food Defense Plan, including a vulnerability assessment and mitigation strategies.

FDA's own analysis identified four process steps as the ones most commonly requiring evaluation across the industry: bulk liquid receiving and loading, liquid storage and handling, secondary ingredient handling, and mixing — facilities must evaluate whether these and other steps in their own process are significant vulnerabilities, and where one is identified, implement mitigation strategies with associated monitoring, corrective actions, and verification.

Small businesses are exempt, and the rule doesn't apply to farms.

93b63909-560d-4414-8cb4-0920241e45a4.name: Food Traceability Rule (FSMA Section 204)
93b63909-560d-4414-8cb4-0920241e45a4.description:
The Food Traceability Rule, issued under FSMA Section 204, targets a narrower problem than the other FSMA rules: not whether food is safe, but whether it can be traced fast enough during an outbreak or recall to prevent illness and limit unnecessary destruction of unaffected product. It applies only to foods on FDA's Food Traceability List (FTL) — a defined set of higher-risk items including soft cheeses, shell eggs, leafy greens, melons, certain seafood, and nut butters — and to the entities that manufacture, process, pack, or hold them.

Covered entities must capture Key Data Elements (KDEs) at specified Critical Tracking Events (CTEs) in the supply chain and maintain those records in a way that lets the full chain reconstruct, from any given point, both where a lot came from and where it went. Records must be produced to FDA within 24 hours of a request, which in practice means most affected facilities need electronic, indexed traceability records rather than paper files that would take days to search.

The rule builds on but doesn't replace the general one-up-one-back recordkeeping obligations already imposed by the Bioterrorism Act; FTL foods carry additional, more prescriptive traceability requirements layered on top. The original compliance date (January 20, 2026) was extended: FDA proposed a 30-month extension in August 2025, and Congress subsequently directed FDA (via a 2026 appropriations act) not to enforce before July 20, 2028 — the current operative date.

52a1eeda-eb18-49b4-bc9f-d51a5ae9c39e.name: Federal Meat Inspection Act (FMIA)
52a1eeda-eb18-49b4-bc9f-d51a5ae9c39e.description:
The Federal Meat Inspection Act is the founding statute for federal meat safety oversight in the United States, giving USDA's Food Safety and Inspection Service (FSIS) — not FDA — jurisdiction over cattle, hogs, sheep, goats, and other amenable livestock species. It requires ante-mortem inspection of live animals and post-mortem inspection of every carcass before it can be labeled and move into commerce.

This inspector-presence model is a structural difference from FDA-regulated food plants, which are inspected periodically rather than continuously — FMIA plants are also outside FDA's Food Safety Modernization Act framework; the parallel food-safety-plan requirement for meat and poultry is the separate USDA HACCP mandate, not PCHF.

ac08bae1-7153-4c36-9af1-3969ebe48a4e.name: Poultry Products Inspection Act (PPIA)
ac08bae1-7153-4c36-9af1-3969ebe48a4e.description:
The Poultry Products Inspection Act extends the same FSIS inspection model established by FMIA to chicken, turkey, duck, and other domesticated poultry — a separate act of Congress in 1957 because poultry slaughter and FMIA's amenable-species list are legally distinct.

FSIS administers PPIA alongside FMIA, and the two acts are typically discussed as a pair for that reason. As with FMIA, PPIA-covered poultry sits outside FDA's FSMA preventive-controls framework — the food-safety-plan obligation runs through USDA HACCP instead.

b69b1236-0674-4d3a-9a57-f37584b74134.name: Egg Products Inspection Act (EPIA)
b69b1236-0674-4d3a-9a57-f37584b74134.description:
The Egg Products Inspection Act covers only egg products that have left the shell — liquid, frozen, and dried egg products produced by breaking plants — and gives FSIS inspection authority over those facilities. Intact shell eggs sold in the carton are regulated by FDA instead, under the general FD&C Act framework; the moment those eggs are broken and turned into a liquid, frozen, or dried product, jurisdiction shifts to USDA.

USDA's Agricultural Marketing Service (AMS) also plays a role here that doesn't exist for meat and poultry: AMS runs a voluntary shell-egg grading program (the familiar Grade AA/A/B marks), distinct from FSIS's mandatory safety inspection of egg products.

18c16d22-6037-4b00-a54b-d91b55a2e001.name: Fair Packaging and Labeling Act (FPLA)
18c16d22-6037-4b00-a54b-d91b55a2e001.description:
The Fair Packaging and Labeling Act is the baseline federal statute governing what a consumer commodity's label must disclose, regardless of what the product actually is. It requires honest, informative labeling — identifying the product and its manufacturer/packer/distributor and stating net quantity of contents — and does not itself set nutrition or safety standards.

Enforcement is split by jurisdiction: FDA enforces FPLA for foods, drugs, cosmetics, and medical devices; FTC enforces it for other consumer commodities not regulated by FDA, such as household goods.

FPLA's label-mechanics requirements sit underneath the more visible nutrition and allergen mandates added later by NLEA and FALCPA — those newer statutes added content requirements on top of the identity and net-quantity baseline FPLA established.

5fedae94-25ee-4237-9dca-d14b6b92b2d5.name: Nutrition Labeling and Education Act (NLEA)
5fedae94-25ee-4237-9dca-d14b6b92b2d5.description:
NLEA amended the FD&C Act to make nutrition labeling mandatory on almost all packaged foods regulated by FDA, ending the prior system where nutrition information was voluntary unless a claim was made. It directed FDA to define a standardized format — the Nutrition Facts panel — with implementing regulations following in 1993 and the panel required on labels starting 1994.

The statute also gave FDA authority to regulate nutrient content claims ("low fat," "high fiber") and health claims linking a nutrient or food to reduced disease risk.

NLEA is a statute, not a fixed format — the actual panel layout has been revised by FDA rulemaking since, most recently the 2016 Nutrition Facts Label update adding an Added Sugars declaration and revised serving sizes. USDA-regulated meat and poultry products fall outside NLEA's scope and are labeled under FMIA/PPIA instead.

b92fd46c-08fa-4a05-95d7-d030b0203096.name: Food Allergen Labeling and Consumer Protection Act (FALCPA)
b92fd46c-08fa-4a05-95d7-d030b0203096.citation: Pub. L. 108-282, Title II; amends 21 U.S.C. § 321
b92fd46c-08fa-4a05-95d7-d030b0203096.description:
FALCPA requires that any of the major food allergens present as an ingredient be plainly declared on the label, either by naming the allergen source in parentheses right after an unclear ingredient term (e.g., "whey (milk)") or by grouping all of them into a single "Contains" statement beneath the ingredient list.

The original law covered eight major allergens — milk, eggs, fish, crustacean shellfish, tree nuts, peanuts, wheat, and soybeans. The FASTER Act amended FALCPA in 2021 to add sesame as the ninth major allergen, with the labeling requirement taking effect January 1, 2023.

For a manufacturer, FALCPA compliance is where allergen labeling and allergen control on the plant floor meet: what the label says must match what an allergen matrix and changeover/cleaning verification actually confirm is or isn't present.

21122a4e-a901-444d-b6d5-8e7a03d67054.name: Bioterrorism Act
21122a4e-a901-444d-b6d5-8e7a03d67054.fullName: Public Health Security and Bioterrorism Preparedness and Response Act
21122a4e-a901-444d-b6d5-8e7a03d67054.description:
The Bioterrorism Act (formally the Public Health Security and Bioterrorism Preparedness and Response Act) directs FDA to require registration of every domestic and foreign facility that manufactures, processes, packs, or holds food for U.S. consumption, and to require prior notice before any food shipment enters the country. Biennial registration renewal (Oct 1 - Dec 31 of even-numbered years) was not part of this original 2002 Act — it was added later by FSMA (2011), which amended FD&C Act § 415(a)(3).

Its recordkeeping provision reaches every FDA-regulated facility's day-to-day operations: each facility must be able to identify the immediate previous source of a food ("one-up") and the immediate subsequent recipient ("one-back"). That one-up-one-back chain is deliberately minimal — it does not require internal lot-level tracking or a shared data format between trading partners — which is the gap FSMA's Food Traceability Rule was later written to close for a defined list of high-risk foods.

FSIS-regulated meat, poultry, and egg products are not subject to this Act — traceability obligations there flow instead from FMIA/PPIA/EPIA and their own recordkeeping rules.

daa53a8a-1ab0-4a41-823b-0bd2779f0ee4.name: HACCP regulation for meat and poultry
daa53a8a-1ab0-4a41-823b-0bd2779f0ee4.description:
9 CFR Part 417 is the regulation that made HACCP mandatory in every USDA-inspected meat and poultry establishment, replacing a purely organoleptic (sight, smell, touch) inspection model with one where the plant itself identifies where contamination is reasonably likely to occur and builds controls around those points. It was published alongside the companion Pathogen Reduction rule ("Pathogen Reduction/HACCP Systems," July 25, 1996), which added microbial testing requirements that work in tandem with the HACCP plan.

The regulation requires every covered plant to conduct a hazard analysis, and if that analysis identifies a food safety hazard reasonably likely to occur, to maintain a written HACCP plan covering it — critical control points, critical limits, monitoring, corrective actions, verification, and recordkeeping. A plant that determines no hazard is reasonably likely to occur at any step still has to document that determination.

FSIS inspectors verify HACCP plans in-plant, checking records, taking measurements, and testing product against the plant's own critical limits, reflecting FSIS's continuous-presence inspection model for meat and poultry.

fields.name: Hazard analysis
fields.description: Written identification of biological, chemical, and physical hazards reasonably likely to occur at each step of the process

fields.name: Critical control points (CCPs)
fields.description: Steps identified where control can be applied to prevent, eliminate, or reduce an identified hazard to an acceptable level

fields.name: Critical limits
fields.description: Maximum or minimum value for a physical, biological, or chemical parameter that must be met at each CCP

fields.name: Monitoring procedures
fields.description: Procedures and frequency for observing and recording whether a CCP is under control

fields.name: Corrective actions
fields.description: Predetermined steps taken when monitoring shows a critical limit has been exceeded

fields.name: Verification activities
fields.description: Methods, including review of monitoring and calibration of instruments, used to confirm the HACCP system is working as intended

fields.name: Recordkeeping
fields.description: Documentation of the hazard analysis, the plan, and all monitoring, corrective action, and verification records

0f215096-8098-4709-b7d5-8c2ecebf3422.name: HACCP regulation for seafood
0f215096-8098-4709-b7d5-8c2ecebf3422.description:
21 CFR Part 123 was FDA's first mandatory HACCP regulation for a food category, published December 18, 1995 and effective December 18, 1997. It applies to seafood processors and importers, following the same hazard analysis / HACCP plan structure used elsewhere in food regulation.

Every seafood processor must conduct a hazard analysis for each product, and where a hazard is reasonably likely to occur, implement a HACCP plan; a processor that determines no hazards are reasonably likely still has to keep that determination on file. Importers have a distinct obligation under this same Part: they must verify that the foreign processor they buy from has an equivalent HACCP system in place.

FDA enforces through periodic inspection rather than continuous in-plant presence, checking plan documentation and monitoring records against actual practice.

fields.name: Hazard analysis
fields.description: Species- and process-specific identification of hazards reasonably likely to occur

fields.name: Critical control points (CCPs)
fields.description: Points where controls (e.g., cooking, freezing, formulation) are applied to control identified hazards

fields.name: Critical limits
fields.description: Measurable parameters that must be met at each CCP

fields.name: Monitoring procedures
fields.description: Procedures confirming CCPs are under control during production

fields.name: Corrective actions
fields.description: Steps taken when a critical limit is not met

fields.name: Verification and recordkeeping
fields.description: Review, calibration, and documentation activities confirming the HACCP system functions as designed

fields.name: Importer verification
fields.description: Procedures importers must use to verify foreign processors operate an equivalent HACCP system

a0ba0e0a-4847-4276-853c-1cc3b93e1c2e.name: HACCP regulation for juice
a0ba0e0a-4847-4276-853c-1cc3b93e1c2e.description:
21 CFR Part 120 makes HACCP mandatory for juice manufacturers, whether the product is sold packaged or fresh-squeezed for immediate sale in some retail settings. It requires a written hazard analysis and, where hazards are reasonably likely to occur, a HACCP plan built around the standard principles used elsewhere in food regulation.

What sets this regulation apart is its specific performance standard: covered juice processes must achieve a 5-log reduction in the pertinent microorganism, a numeric target most plants meet through pasteurization, though the rule doesn't mandate a specific technology. Juice sold with a warning label about the absence of that treatment is a permitted alternative compliance path.

As with seafood, importers of juice must verify their foreign suppliers operate an equivalent HACCP program, and FDA enforcement runs through inspection of plans and monitoring records. Final rule published January 19, 2001, with phased effective dates (large businesses 2002, small 2003, very small 2004).

fields.name: Hazard analysis
fields.description: Identification of hazards reasonably likely to occur in the specific juice and process

fields.name: Critical control points (CCPs)
fields.description: Process step(s), typically the pathogen-reduction/pasteurization step, where control is applied

fields.name: 5-log pathogen reduction standard
fields.description: Numeric performance requirement that the process achieve a 5-log reduction in the pertinent microorganism

fields.name: Critical limits and monitoring
fields.description: Parameters (time, temperature, dose) and monitoring procedures confirming the reduction standard is met

fields.name: Corrective actions and verification
fields.description: Steps taken on deviation and activities confirming ongoing system effectiveness

fields.name: Recordkeeping
fields.description: Documentation of hazard analysis, plan, monitoring, and verification

fields.name: Warning label alternative
fields.description: Permitted compliance path allowing sale of juice without the full pathogen-reduction process if labeled with the required warning statement

2adc08a2-5082-4f11-9890-73c5c1bb546e.name: National Bioengineered Food Disclosure Standard (NBFDS)
2adc08a2-5082-4f11-9890-73c5c1bb546e.description:
The National Bioengineered Food Disclosure Standard requires most food manufacturers to tell consumers when a product contains bioengineered (BE) ingredients — the regulatory term for what's commonly called GMO. USDA's Agricultural Marketing Service administers the standard, not FDA.

A covered manufacturer discloses via one of several formats: on-package text, the USDA-designed BE symbol, a QR code or other digital link, or a phone-number option. Highly refined ingredients with no detectable modified genetic material are generally exempt, even if the source crop was bioengineered.

Enacted 2016 (amending the Agricultural Marketing Act of 1946); final rule published December 2018; implementation began January 2020 (2021 for small manufacturers); mandatory compliance began January 1, 2022.

719f7e9a-1a25-4253-93f4-b03c648b5265.name: National Organic Program (NOP)
719f7e9a-1a25-4253-93f4-b03c648b5265.description:
The National Organic Program, run by USDA's Agricultural Marketing Service, is the accreditation and enforcement system behind the word "organic" on a food label. Farms and handling facilities that want to sell or process food as organic must be certified by a USDA-accredited third-party certifying agent.

NOP defines a labeling hierarchy: "100% Organic" (every agricultural ingredient is organic), "Organic" (at least 95% of agricultural content is organic), and "Made with Organic [ingredient/category]" (at least 70% organic content, up to 3 ingredients/categories listed). Statutory basis is the Organic Foods Production Act of 1990; final rule published December 2000, effective October 2002.

Because certification and labeling are handled by a separate USDA agency (AMS) from the one that oversees meat and poultry inspection (FSIS) or general food safety (FDA), a single product can be subject to NOP labeling rules on top of whichever agency's core food-safety jurisdiction it falls under.

2b3c4d5e-6f7a-4b8c-9d0e-1f2a3b4c5d6e.name: Nutrition Facts Label Final Rule (2016 revision)
2b3c4d5e-6f7a-4b8c-9d0e-1f2a3b4c5d6e.description:
This is FDA's most significant redesign of the Nutrition Facts panel since NLEA established it. It's issued under the same statutory authority as the original Nutrition Facts requirement but as a standalone final rule, so it's tracked separately here because it changed content and format requirements, not the underlying legal mandate to label at all.

The headline changes: a new Added Sugars declaration, updated Daily Values, a larger/bolder typeface for the calorie count, and revised serving-size reference amounts intended to better reflect actual consumption.

Compliance dates (extended from the original rule by a 2018 rule) were January 1, 2020 for manufacturers with $10M+ in annual food sales and January 1, 2021 for smaller manufacturers. Getting this wrong means printing an out-of-date or noncompliant label, which turns into a label-artwork revision cycle across every affected SKU.

3c4d5e6f-7a8b-4c9d-0e1f-2a3b4c5d6e7f.name: Front-of-Package Nutrition Information (proposed rule)
3c4d5e6f-7a8b-4c9d-0e1f-2a3b4c5d6e7f.description:
FDA proposed this rule to require a standardized "Nutrition Info Box" on the front of most packaged food, in addition to the existing Nutrition Facts panel on the back or side. The stated goal is to let shoppers see at a glance whether a product is high, medium, or low in saturated fat, sodium, and added sugars — the three nutrients most tied to diet-related chronic disease.

This is a proposed rule, not a final one — it has gone through public comment (which closed July 2025) but has not been finalized, and both its ultimate content and its effective/compliance dates remain unsettled. FDA's stated priorities target a final rule around spring 2026, but manufacturers should not treat any specific design or deadline from the proposal as locked in.

Tracked here as an in-flight regulation the way FSMA 204's compliance-date extension was tracked before becoming binding — the record should be revisited once (and if) FDA issues a final rule.

a36806f7-5f25-486d-b4f0-dc3a8e6c4210.name: BRCGS Global Standard for Food Safety
a36806f7-5f25-486d-b4f0-dc3a8e6c4210.citation: BRCGS Global Standard for Food Safety, Issue 9
a36806f7-5f25-486d-b4f0-dc3a8e6c4210.description:
The BRCGS Global Standard for Food Safety is a private certification standard (formerly the British Retail Consortium standard, now branded BRCGS) covering food safety management systems, HACCP, and site standards. It is recognized by the Global Food Safety Initiative (GFSI), meaning major retailers and foodservice buyers accept a passing BRCGS audit as evidence a supplier's food safety system meets a common benchmark.

Certification is issued following an audit by an accredited third-party certification body, not by BRCGS itself or by any government agency. Issue 9 was published August 2022, with certification audits against it beginning February 2023.

Because it is privately administered, BRCGS certification operates alongside, not instead of, government food safety law — a US facility holding BRCGS certification is still separately subject to FDA's or FSIS's regulatory requirements. It is widely required by UK and European retailers as a supplier-qualification condition.

2cda1fd4-2d76-488e-a222-04d4b30c88f8.name: IFS Food Standard
2cda1fd4-2d76-488e-a222-04d4b30c88f8.description:
IFS Food is a private certification standard developed by International Featured Standards (IFS), assessing food safety and process/product quality across several processing scopes. Like BRCGS, it is recognized by the Global Food Safety Initiative (GFSI), so a passing IFS audit is accepted by participating retailers as proof of an adequate food safety and quality system. It is not a government regulation; certification is voluntary, though many European and increasingly North American retail customers require it as a condition of doing business.

Version 8 became mandatory for all certified companies on January 1, 2024, and achieved GFSI recognition against the 2020 GFSI Benchmarking Requirements in September 2024.

A facility pursuing IFS Food certification in the US still operates under FDA's or FSIS's regulatory framework independent of the certification; IFS does not substitute for or exempt a site from PCHF, HACCP, or other applicable federal food safety law.
